title: Dennis and Dee Go on Welfare

jokes.text: My dad's a meth dealer. - Oh, oh, no! - My daddy's in prison! - My daddy used to give hot shots to prostitutes.
jokes.setup: Characters complaining about working for their dad
jokes.punchline: Each character one-ups with increasingly criminal/disturbing parental occupations
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalating structure revealing each character's terrible family background. Classic Sunny dark humor that defines these awful characters.

jokes.text: Feel sorry for me! I got the grout.
jokes.setup: Other characters dramatically competing over traumatic backstories
jokes.punchline: Charlie deflates the moment by focusing on mundane grout work
jokes.explanation: Charlie's oblivious response perfectly deflates the dramatic moment. Great character-specific humor.

jokes.text: Later, bitches
jokes.setup: Frank is leaving his adult children
jokes.punchline: Uses casual, dismissive slang like a teenager ditching friends
jokes.explanation: Frank's inappropriate casual tone when abandoning his children. Good character moment but fairly standard.

jokes.text: Signing us up for unemployment benefits
jokes.setup: Dee asks what he's doing after their goals speech
jokes.punchline: Instead of pursuing dreams, he's immediately signing up for government assistance
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate contradiction of their lofty aspirations. Good structure showing their true nature.

jokes.text: Well, I guess I could use this time to get my acting career off the ground.
jokes.setup: Dennis talking about golden opportunities and putting minds to work
jokes.punchline: Dee's delusional acting aspirations despite clearly having no talent
jokes.explanation: Sets up Dee's delusional character trait that will pay off throughout the episode.

jokes.text: The old Dennis would have said, 'Dee, your sad little acting ship sailed a decade ago. It's pathetic.' But the new Dennis says that it's not my place to kill your pipe dreams.
jokes.setup: Dennis claiming to be supportive of Dee's dreams
jokes.punchline: He immediately insults her dreams while pretending to be supportive
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dennis manipulation - he's cruel while pretending to be kind. Excellent structure and character work.

jokes.text: 400 a week. That's more than we made at the bar.
jokes.setup: Characters excited about unemployment benefits amount
jokes.punchline: Reveals how little money their bar actually makes
jokes.explanation: Good reveal about their business incompetence. Economic and character-driven.

jokes.text: Goddamn it! Why can you not lift your end? I don't know, dude. Let me figure out what is happening.
jokes.setup: Mac and Charlie trying to move a pool table
jokes.punchline: They're completely incompetent at basic physical tasks
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy. Functional but not particularly clever or original.

jokes.text: Of course I'm lifting with my back. I think your end is lighter. The balls have rolled down to this end.
jokes.setup: Struggling with pool table physics
jokes.punchline: Charlie's absurd logic about pool balls affecting weight distribution
jokes.explanation: Charlie's absurd reasoning is character-specific and mildly clever.

jokes.text: Look at me. I'm broken, dude. Look, I'm...
jokes.setup: Mac claiming Frank is breaking Charlie's spirit
jokes.punchline: Charlie's pathetic agreement and defeated posture
jokes.explanation: Charlie's sad desperation is both pathetic and funny. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Oh, living the dream, bro. Living the dream
jokes.setup: Mac asks what they're doing
jokes.punchline: Dennis sarcastically says 'living the dream' while visibly drunk and disheveled in afternoon
jokes.explanation: Standard ironic response. The visual of day-drinking elevates it slightly but it's fairly predictable.

jokes.text: I'm gonna be a veterinarian. And I am gonna move to New York, and I'm gonna be on Broadway.
jokes.setup: Characters explaining their career aspirations while drunk in afternoon
jokes.punchline: Completely unrealistic goals given their current state and abilities
jokes.explanation: Their delusional aspirations contrasted with being drunk at 1 PM is classic character comedy.

jokes.text: What? It's 1:00 in the afternoon, and you're piss-ass drunk.
jokes.setup: Dennis and Dee explaining their grand plans
jokes.punchline: Mac points out the obvious contradiction with their current state
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man observation that highlights the absurdity. Economical and effective.

jokes.text: Well, it's a slow process, man. It takes a little time. But we trust the system
jokes.setup: Called out for day drinking while supposedly pursuing careers
jokes.punchline: Dennis frames alcoholism as part of a methodical career plan
jokes.explanation: Good Dennis rationalization - turning dysfunction into a philosophy. Well-crafted character moment.

jokes.text: Is that a new watch, dude? It is. It's awesome. And I got a Jesus chain.
jokes.setup: Characters claiming to be working toward goals
jokes.punchline: They're actually just buying jewelry with government money
jokes.explanation: Shows their priorities and shallow nature. The Jesus chain detail is particularly good character work.

jokes.text: Once it runs out, we're gonna go on welfare, dude.
jokes.setup: Mac warning that unemployment runs out
jokes.punchline: They've already planned to escalate to welfare
jokes.explanation: Good escalation that shows they've thought this through in the worst possible way.

jokes.text: Once it runs out, we're gonna go on welfare, dude
jokes.setup: Mac warns that unemployment runs out
jokes.punchline: Dennis already has a plan to escalate to the next level of government assistance
jokes.explanation: Shows Dennis has thought this through and planned to game multiple systems. Good escalation.

jokes.text: You want to play this game? We gonna play this game now?
jokes.setup: Mac criticizing their welfare plan
jokes.punchline: Dennis treats reasonable criticism as a personal challenge
jokes.explanation: Dennis's narcissistic reaction to criticism is in character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Right, right. So what's the vig? The vig? Yeah, man. You know, what's... what's the vig on this action here? Do you even know what 'vig' means?
jokes.setup: Charlie trying to sound street-smart about money laundering
jokes.punchline: Frank calling out that Charlie doesn't know what he's talking about
jokes.explanation: Charlie's fake street knowledge getting called out is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: We hire a couple people, pay them nothing, and get them to do all of our work for us! We pay them nothing! That's incredible!
jokes.setup: Mac explaining the welfare work program
jokes.punchline: They completely misunderstand it as literal slavery
jokes.explanation: Their complete misunderstanding of the program is both dark and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Respect is the name of the game. Respect is number one! / It's the name of almost every game / Because we understand the plight of the worker
jokes.setup: Frank emphasizes treating workers with respect
jokes.punchline: Mac and Charlie enthusiastically agree while completely missing the point
jokes.explanation: They parrot Frank's words while planning exploitation. The 'name of almost every game' is particularly absurd.

jokes.text: I'm good. Go get us some slaves.
jokes.setup: Mac and Charlie talking about respect and treating workers well
jokes.punchline: Frank cuts through their bullshit with casual cruelty
jokes.explanation: Frank's casual cruelty delivered with perfect economy. Dark but effective character moment.

jokes.text: There have been slaves of many different races. There's been Jewish slaves. I mean, uh, Italian, uh...
jokes.setup: Charlie trying to justify using the word 'slaves'
jokes.punchline: His historically tone-deaf explanation makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Charlie's oblivious racism creates uncomfortable cringe humor that's characteristic of the show.

jokes.text: I'm not saying that because he's black. He's asleep in his chair.
jokes.setup: Mac accusing Charlie of racial profiling
jokes.punchline: Charlie's defense actually makes logical sense for once
jokes.explanation: Rare moment where Charlie's logic is actually sound, subverting expectations.

jokes.text: That's like reverse affirmative action, dude. You could cheat yourself out of a good slave here.
jokes.setup: Mac suggesting they talk to a different person
jokes.punchline: Charlie's completely backwards understanding of affirmative action
jokes.explanation: Charlie's confused social commentary is both absurd and uncomfortably revealing of his worldview.

jokes.text: Like weight, power... Something to just 'expediate' the whole process.
jokes.setup: Asking for information about welfare recipients
jokes.punchline: Treating people like livestock while mispronouncing 'expedite'
jokes.explanation: The mispronunciation adds to Mac's ignorance while the request itself is horrifyingly dehumanizing.

jokes.text: Drug addicts, mentally disabled, people with dependents... that sort of thing / Mac, we've got it covered, okay? Dee, your bicycle helmet
jokes.setup: Mac lists who qualifies for welfare
jokes.punchline: Dennis reveals Dee has been wearing a bicycle helmet to appear disabled
jokes.explanation: Great setup/payoff revealing their fraud scheme. The visual of Dee in a helmet is perfect.

jokes.text: That is an awesome helmet.
jokes.setup: Dee wearing a bicycle helmet to appear mentally disabled
jokes.punchline: Mac's genuine admiration for the clearly inappropriate helmet
jokes.explanation: Mac's oblivious reaction to Dee's offensive costume attempt is appropriately stupid.

jokes.text: Hi. Um, I'm a recovering crackhead. This is my retarded sister that I take care of. I'd like some welfare, please.
jokes.setup: Dennis and Dee trying to qualify for welfare
jokes.punchline: Their casual, offensive lies delivered with fake sincerity
jokes.explanation: Their shameless fraud attempt is perfectly cringe and shows their complete lack of moral boundaries.

jokes.text: Well, can't you see how retarded she is?
jokes.setup: Being asked for medical paperwork
jokes.punchline: Dennis doubling down on the offensive charade
jokes.explanation: Dennis's persistence in the lie shows his complete lack of shame or awareness.

jokes.text: Oh, they want to play rough? We can play rough. Let's go buy some crack.
jokes.setup: Being denied welfare due to lack of documentation
jokes.punchline: Dee's solution is to actually become a crack addict
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation showing how far they'll go. The casual delivery of such an extreme solution is perfectly Sunny.

jokes.text: Uh, one rock of crack. One crack... A crack rock. Is that enough? Is one crack rock enough? How much would you recommend for a first-time user?
jokes.setup: Dennis trying to buy drugs
jokes.punchline: He approaches it like a polite customer service interaction
jokes.explanation: Dennis's privileged politeness in a drug deal is hilariously inappropriate and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: One crack rock. Is that enough? Is one crack rock enough? / How much would you recommend for a first-time user?
jokes.setup: Buying drugs to fake addiction
jokes.punchline: They treat drug dealer like a helpful customer service representative
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist moment treating crack purchase like shopping for wine. Perfect privileged naivety.

jokes.text: That should be about 200. Thanks for being so kind. And patient.
jokes.setup: Completing the drug transaction
jokes.punchline: Dennis treating the dealer like a helpful shopkeeper
jokes.explanation: Continues the polite drug purchase joke. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: This one. It's got to be that guy. Don't say 'this one.'
jokes.setup: Mac pointing at someone while Charlie warns about sensitivity
jokes.punchline: Mac immediately does the exact thing Charlie warned against
jokes.explanation: Mac's immediate failure to follow Charlie's advice shows his obtuseness.

jokes.text: I'm gonna get nice and drunk, play some video games until my eyes bleed / Me too. I want to play Charlie
jokes.setup: Having workers do all the labor
jokes.punchline: Frank wants to copy Charlie's ridiculous lifestyle exactly
jokes.explanation: Frank treating Charlie like a lifestyle to emulate. Good character moment but not exceptional.

jokes.text: The three of us go outside, throw the pigskin around, play some two-hand touch. I'll teach you how to play. I already know how to play. Not properly though.
jokes.setup: Frank suggesting they play football
jokes.punchline: Frank's condescending assumption that Charlie needs teaching
jokes.explanation: Frank's patronizing father figure behavior is uncomfortable and appropriately cringe.

jokes.text: Frank's been hiding all of his money in my account. Uh, it's got something to do with the vig. What's a vig?
jokes.setup: Charlie revealing he has access to Frank's money
jokes.punchline: Still doesn't know what 'vig' means despite using the term
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier confusion while setting up their money theft plot.

jokes.text: Here's some for you! Here's some for you! And here's some for you!
jokes.setup: Charlie and Mac spending Frank's money at bars/clubs
jokes.punchline: Charlie throwing money around like a big spender
jokes.explanation: Charlie acting like a big shot is mildly funny but predictable.

jokes.text: It's 4:00. I'm freezing and I'm sweating all at once. I think I may have peed in my pants.
jokes.setup: Dee and Dennis waking up from drug use
jokes.punchline: Graphic description of withdrawal symptoms
jokes.explanation: The physical consequences of their plan create appropriate discomfort and karma.

jokes.text: We missed our appointment by an hour and a day
jokes.setup: They think they're only an hour late
jokes.punchline: Dennis's formal way of saying they slept through an entire day
jokes.explanation: Good Dennis phrasing making their complete failure sound bureaucratic. Nice wordplay.

jokes.text: It was yesterday. We missed our appointment by an hour and a day.
jokes.setup: Dennis thinking they're only an hour late
jokes.punchline: They lost an entire day to drug use
jokes.explanation: Good escalation revealing how badly their plan backfired. Economical and effective.

jokes.text: Dennis, when you become a veterinarian, will you buy me some new head shots? Hey! Hey, get out of here, you piece of shit! Yeah, I'll buy you stuff.
jokes.setup: Dee asking about headshots while they're clearly drug addicts
jokes.punchline: Dennis yelling at someone then casually agreeing to buy things
jokes.explanation: Their delusional planning while clearly in crisis is both sad and funny.

jokes.text: I'm definitely gonna buy a bunch of crack.
jokes.setup: Dennis promising to buy Dee things when successful
jokes.punchline: His immediate priority is more drugs
jokes.explanation: Dennis's honest admission about his priorities is darkly funny and economical.

jokes.text: Holding... Do you have any money? Please? I just need a little bit of money.
jokes.setup: Mac asking if they're 'holding' (drugs)
jokes.punchline: Dennis desperately begging for money instead
jokes.explanation: Dennis's fall from privilege to desperate beggar is appropriately pathetic.

jokes.text: You work hard, you don't drink... How'd you end up on welfare?
jokes.setup: Frank observing his workers' good qualities
jokes.punchline: His confusion about why good people would need assistance
jokes.explanation: Frank's privileged confusion about poverty is both revealing and darkly comic.

jokes.text: Well, it's a vicious cycle, Mr. Frank. One cannot support a family on a minimum-wage position.
jokes.setup: Frank asking about welfare need
jokes.punchline: Worker gives articulate explanation of systemic poverty
jokes.explanation: The worker's intelligent response contrasts with the gang's ignorance about real problems.

jokes.text: We got a big problem here, buddy / The services have already been rendered. We gotta pay these girls / I'm calling Hector / Don't call Hector or Wallace or any other large pimp
jokes.setup: Charlie's account is frozen
jokes.punchline: They owe money to prostitutes and are afraid of their pimps
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how their schemes always spiral into criminal problems.

jokes.text: Did somebody get addicted to crack? I sweat uncontrollably! I can't get somebody to have sex with me unless I pay for it!
jokes.setup: Mac mocking Dennis for getting addicted
jokes.punchline: Dennis's graphic oversharing about his problems
jokes.explanation: Dennis's desperate admission is both pathetic and reveals his fallen state.

jokes.text: I sweat uncontrollably! / I can't get somebody to have sex with me unless I pay for it!
jokes.setup: They're listing their problems
jokes.punchline: Mac admits to needing prostitutes in increasingly desperate tone
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of pathetic admissions. Mac's confession is particularly sad.

jokes.text: Slaves are gone. They were bringing me down. Plus, you know they don't drink?
jokes.setup: Charlie asking about the welfare workers
jokes.punchline: Frank fired them for being too responsible
jokes.explanation: Frank's twisted logic and priorities are perfectly in character and darkly funny.

jokes.text: This shows leadership. I am promoting you to management. That's why I did it.
jokes.setup: Frank confronting Charlie about stealing money
jokes.punchline: Frank rewards the theft as showing initiative
jokes.explanation: Frank's backwards moral system where theft equals leadership is perfectly twisted.

jokes.text: You get dick, because you are a follower and a thief. But how come Charlie? Not fair.
jokes.setup: Mac expecting a reward for also stealing
jokes.punchline: Frank's arbitrary distinction between Charlie and Mac's identical behavior
jokes.explanation: Frank's inconsistent logic and Mac's whining create good character-based humor.

jokes.text: Why would you do this to us, Dad? / Because you are crackheads, children
jokes.setup: Dennis pleads with Frank using 'Dad'
jokes.punchline: Frank's matter-of-fact explanation delivered paternally
jokes.explanation: Perfect father-son dynamic with Frank being brutally honest. Great economy and delivery.

jokes.text: Because you are crackheads, children. Yeah. Crack. Crack. Crack. Crack.
jokes.setup: Dennis and Dee asking why they're being punished
jokes.punchline: Frank's blunt assessment while they pathetically agree
jokes.explanation: The repetition of 'crack' shows how far they've fallen and provides a dark comedic ending.

tmdb_overview: After quitting their job due to Frank's continuing interference, Dennis and Dee decide to seek alternative income by going on welfare. Meanwhile, Mac and Charlie must now bear the consequences of their friends’ departures from the business.
